================================================================================
## Blog: Your First itch.io Page for a Vibe-Coded Game
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/first-itch-io-page-vibe-coded-game
Source: src/content/blog/en/first-itch-io-page-vibe-coded-game.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Metadata, embeds, and pricing psychology for browser-first indies—without burying the play button.

**itch.io** remains the friendliest home for **experimental browser games**. Your job is to reduce friction: **play in one click**, understand controls in **five seconds**, and trust you with **updates**.

## Page structure

- **Title + genre tag** in first line of description.
- **Embed** above the fold on desktop; mobile gets a giant **Play** link.
- **Controls** section duplicated near embed and in README.

## Pricing

Pay-what-you-want with a **suggested price** signals confidence. Free with tip jar works for portfolio builds—say so honestly.

## Builds

For HTML5, zip the **smallest** `dist/` that runs standalone. Test unzip → open `index.html` locally (or host static) before upload.

## Devlogs

Short, dated entries beat manifestos. “**0.2 — fixed Safari audio**” builds follower trust.

## FAQ

**Do I need a trailer?** Helpful; a **30s capture** is enough at first.

**Cross-link hub listing?** Yes—link your [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) entry and [developer profile](/developers).

## Next steps

- Join a [game jam](/game-jams) to force a deadline.
- Compare AI stacks in [AI tools](/ai-tools).

---

*YGG Play helps indies meet players where they already browse.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Turn a Game Idea into a One-Page Design Doc AI Tools Understand
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/game-idea-to-one-page-design-doc-for-ai-tools
Source: src/content/blog/en/game-idea-to-one-page-design-doc-for-ai-tools.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Constraints, loops, and acceptance tests—so your assistant stops freelancing your design.

Great prompts start as **boring documents**. One page forces **clarity**; clarity reduces hallucinated features.

## Sections (in order)

1. **Elevator** — 2 sentences.
2. **Core loop** — bullet timeline of one round.
3. **Inputs & platforms** — keyboard, touch, gamepad?
4. **Entities** — player, enemies, pickups; max counts.
5. **Progression** — what changes between rounds?
6. **Non-goals** — explicit cuts.
7. **Acceptance tests** — verifiable sentences.

## Example acceptance test

> Given two players join room `ABCD`, when player one presses **Space**, round starts within **200ms** and timer counts down from **60**.

## Why non-goals matter

AI fills silence. Tell it **what not to build** (no accounts, no shop, no story mode).

## FAQ

**Length?** One page, 12pt, no appendix. Link art references instead of embedding essays.

**Versioning?** `design-v3.md` in repo; mention version in prompts.

## Next steps

- Ship something listable on [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games).
- Meet collaborators via [developers](/developers).

---

*YGG Play turns vague ideas into shippable constraints.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Why Game Jams Hit Different in the Vibe-Coding Era
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/game-jams-vibe-coding-era
Source: src/content/blog/en/game-jams-vibe-coding-era.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Deadlines still matter—but prototypes arrive denser, and judges read tool tags with more curiosity than suspicion.

**Game jams** always rewarded **clarity of vision** over raw engineering hours. Vibe-coding tools tilt the curve: more teams reach **playable vertical slices**, which raises the bar for **polish, narrative framing, and originality**.

## What is improving

- **Iteration velocity** during the first 12 hours—UI and scaffolding arrive faster.
- **Cross-discipline solo entries** where one person covers code + rough art passes.
- **Documentation**: assistants help README and control schemes appear earlier.

## What still hurts

- **Integration bugs** when generated modules disagree.
- **Performance surprises** on low-end laptops if testing is desktop-only.
- **Judging fairness** when disclosure of AI use varies by region and platform.

## Community norms we support

- **Say what you used**—players are curious, not allergic.
- **Credit human collaborators** explicitly.
- **Separate “AI-assisted” from “AI-generated art”** when possible.

## Builders: treat jams as marketing labs

A jam build is a **time-boxed trailer** for your skills. Link it from your [developer profile](/developers) and consider a hub listing on [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) if the URL stays stable.

## FAQ

**Are AI games “less legit”?** Players reward **fun**; historians reward **context**. Provide both.

**Where to find jams?** Start at [game jams](/game-jams) on Vibecode.game and follow organizers you trust.

---

*YGG Play champions jams as on-ramps for new listers.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Build a Tiny Multiplayer Game with Cursor in One Evening
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/how-to-multiplayer-game-cursor-two-hours
Source: src/content/blog/en/how-to-multiplayer-game-cursor-two-hours.mdx
================================================================================

Description: A practical evening sprint: lobby, shared state, and a playable loop—using Cursor to move fast without drowning in boilerplate.

You do not need a perfect netcode stack to **learn multiplayer** or to ship a jam-sized proof. You need a **tight scope**, a single transport choice, and a workflow that keeps you coding instead of debating architecture. This guide assumes you are comfortable with JavaScript or TypeScript, have [Cursor](https://cursor.com) installed, and want a **two-hour ceiling** you can defend when motivation is high but time is not.

## What you will have at the end

- A **browser game** where two tabs (or two devices on your LAN) see the same world state update in near real time.
- A **minimal message protocol** (JSON over WebSockets) you can explain in one slide.
- A **checklist** for turning the prototype into something you would list on [Vibecode.game](/vibe-coded-games) with screenshots and a clear “how it was built” story.

## Hour zero: lock the design

Pick **one verb** for players: *dodge*, *tag*, *collect*, or *push*. Cap interactions at **three inputs** (e.g. WASD). One arena, no menus, no accounts. If you cannot describe the win condition in **one sentence**, cut scope until you can.

| Scope lever | Tight (good) | Too wide (cut) |
|-------------|--------------|----------------|
| Players | 2–8 | MMO-style persistence |
| State | One shared JSON object | Per-entity physics engine |
| Auth | Room code in URL | Accounts + ELO |

## Hour one: server + socket skeleton

Use a tiny Node server (`ws` or your framework’s WebSocket helper). **Rooms** map to random four-letter codes. On join, push the current state snapshot; after that, broadcast **only deltas** you care about (player position, score ticks).

Cursor shines when you **paste your constraints** into the chat: transport (WebSocket), message shape, and “no database.” Ask for a single-file server plus a matching client module, then refactor once it runs.

**Milestone:** two clients connect; server logs two sockets; you can broadcast a `{ type: 'ping' }` and see it in both tabs.

## Hour two: game loop + playtest

Run the simulation at a fixed tick (e.g. 10–20 Hz) or event-driven if movement is sparse. **Client prediction** can wait—start with server-authoritative positions to avoid desync rabbit holes in a short sprint.

Playtest with a friend or a second browser profile. Watch for **message storms** (sending full state 60 times a second) and **NaN positions** from bad input handling.

## Common mistakes in two-hour multiplayer sprints

1. **Starting with matchmaking** instead of a hard-coded room.
2. **Encoding everything as strings** with no schema—add a one-page TypeScript `type` for messages.
3. **Skipping disconnect handling**—at minimum, remove stale players server-side.

## FAQ

**Is WebSocket always the right choice?** For browser prototypes with continuous state, usually yes. For turn-based games, HTTP + polling can be enough.

**Can AI tools replace understanding netcode?** No—but they accelerate boilerplate if *you* keep the scope and invariants clear.

**Where do I list the shipped build?** When you have a stable URL, add your game to the community catalog and polish your [developer profile](/developers) so players see your stack and tools.

## Next steps

- Compare AI coding stacks in our [AI tools directory](/ai-tools).
- Plan a jam submission using [game jams](/game-jams) as your deadline.
- Browse [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) for pacing and presentation ideas from other listers.

---

*YGG Play publishes hands-on guides for builders who ship browser-first games with modern AI-assisted workflows.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Local Co-op in an Afternoon: Cursor + Phaser 3
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/local-co-op-prototype-cursor-phaser-afternoon
Source: src/content/blog/en/local-co-op-prototype-cursor-phaser-afternoon.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Shared keyboard, simple physics, and a finish line you can hit before dinner.

**Local co-op** avoids networking complexity while still teaching **input routing**, **collision layers**, and **game feel**. Phaser 3 gives you scenes, arcade physics, and sprite plumbing—Cursor helps you avoid boilerplate traps if you describe **inputs and win rules** crisply.

## Afternoon schedule

| Block | Focus |
|-------|-------|
| :00–:45 | Scene boot, two sprites, tilemap or bounds |
| :45–1:30 | Controls (WASD + arrows), collisions, score |
| 1:30–2:30 | Juice: screenshake lite, sfx hooks, pause |
| 2:30–3:00 | Build + static deploy smoke test |

## Input map

Explicitly bind **separate key sets**. On gamepad day two, you will thank yourself for clean abstraction (`p1`, `p2` objects).

## Physics honesty

Start **arcade**; swap to matter only if puzzles need it. AI often over-engineers physics—reject that diff.

## Deploy

Phaser bundles cleanly with Vite. Ensure **asset paths** work from `/` and from nested routes if your host uses subpaths.

## FAQ

**Controllers?** Defer unless afternoon scope creeps—keyboard proves the loop.

**Online later?** Finish hot-seat first; read our [multiplayer Cursor guide](/blog/how-to-multiplayer-game-cursor-two-hours).

## Next steps

- List when playable on [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games).
- Tag tools on [AI tools](/ai-tools).

---

*YGG Play celebrates small slices that ship.*

================================================================================
## Blog: How Niche Game News Finds Sources—and How to Be One
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/niche-game-news-backlinks-source-guide
Source: src/content/blog/en/niche-game-news-backlinks-source-guide.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Be quotable, fast to verify, and generous with embeds: the boring playbook for earning links.

**Backlinks** follow **credibility**. Niche outlets want **primary sources**: builds that run, founders who reply, and facts that check out.

## Make a /press page

- **Factsheet** (bullet studio, genre, platforms, price).
- **Logo & screenshots** ZIP.
- **Contact** with SLA you can keep (e.g. 48h).

## Be quotable

Share **measurable** claims: load time, player count order-of-magnitude, jam placement—avoid unverifiable superlatives.

## Embeds and exclusives

Offer **GIF-first** assets; journalists live on deadlines.

## Ethical outreach

- Personalize **one** sentence proving you read their column.
- No mass mail merge guilt trips.

## Tie-ins

List where you **ship** on [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games); point to [developers](/developers) for bio depth.

## FAQ

**Follow up?** Once, politely; then move on.

**Harassment?** Never—reputation is a long game.

---

*YGG Play helps builders earn attention without burning bridges.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Prepare Your Indie Game for a Community Hub Listing
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/prepare-indie-game-for-community-hub-listing
Source: src/content/blog/en/prepare-indie-game-for-community-hub-listing.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Screenshots, honest tool tags, and a ‘making of’ blurb players trust—before you hit publish.

Hubs like **Vibecode.game** work when listings answer three questions in **ten seconds**: *What is it?* *Can I play now?* *Why should I trust this build?* AI-assisted development makes disclosure **more** important, not less—players celebrate craft when you are transparent.

## The five-minute scroll test

Open your listing on a phone you have never used before. If the **first screen** does not show gameplay, fix that before writing lore.

## Media package

- **Cover:** 16:9, readable at thumbnail size—no micro-text.
- **3–5 GIFs or short clips** showing **input → outcome**.
- **Still of controls** on first-run overlay.

## Honest tool tags

List **primary** tools (engine, major AI assistants, art pipeline). Players use tags to **filter** and learn. Misleading tags burn trust.

## Copy that converts

Structure:

1. **One-line hook** (genre + twist).
2. **How to play** in bullets.
3. **What is vibe-coded** about it—constraints, jams, stack.
4. **Roadmap** if early access.

## Performance truthfulness

State target devices. “**Desktop Chrome** recommended” is fine; silent failure on mobile is not.

## Table: listing readiness

| Item | Ready? |
|------|--------|
| Stable play URL | ☐ |
| Credits + license for assets | ☐ |
| Privacy if you collect data | ☐ |
| Contact or Discord for bugs | ☐ |

## FAQ

**Do I need a trailer?** Nice, not mandatory—**GIF loop** often beats a padded trailer.

**Where do I start?** Open [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games), study listings you admire, then polish your [developer profile](/developers).

## Next steps

- Explore [AI tools](/ai-tools) to tag accurately.
- Join momentum via [game jams](/game-jams).

---

*YGG Play helps players find AI-built games worth their time.*

================================================================================
## Blog: From Prompt to Playable: A Lovable-First Web Game Workflow
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/prompt-to-playable-lovable-web-game-workflow
Source: src/content/blog/en/prompt-to-playable-lovable-web-game-workflow.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Use Lovable’s UI velocity for scaffolding, then tighten gameplay in focused passes—without losing your creative north star.

**Lovable**-style builders reward **clear prompts** and punish vague dreams. Treat the tool like a senior UI engineer who needs acceptance criteria, not a mind reader who invents your game design.

## Frame the vertical slice

Write a **one-paragraph design brief** before touching the generator:

- Camera perspective (top-down, side, isometric-lite).
- Win/lose in one line.
- Input scheme (touch-first vs keyboard-first).

Attach **reference games** as comparisons (“*Downwell*-style gravity” not “something cool”).

## Structure prompts in layers

1. **Layout shell** — navigation, canvas region, HUD placeholders.
2. **State module** — score, lives, timers as plain data structures.
3. **Interaction pass** — collisions, spawning, juice (particles later).

Ask for **file names** explicitly so you can navigate diffs. Reject drive-by rewrites; insist on **patch-sized** changes when something already works.

## Playtest cadence

Every **20 minutes**, play one round on **phone + desktop**. Input latency shows up differently. Note framerate dips before adding shaders.

## Integration with hand-written code

Export or sync to your repo early. **Lock dependencies** and add a tiny unit test around scoring if you plan competitive leaderboards later.

## Table: prompt smells vs strong prompts

| Weak | Strong |
|------|--------|
| “Make it fun” | “Increase difficulty every 30s by +10% spawn rate cap 200%” |
| “Fix bugs” | “On Safari iOS, touchmove prevents scroll; lock body overflow during play” |
| “Better art” | “Replace circles with 32×32 placeholder tiles; keep hitboxes AABB” |

## Pitfalls

- **Over-automating juice** before core loop is tight.
- **Hidden magic numbers**—centralize tuning in one config object.
- **Skipping sound off** during meetings—mute is a feature.

## FAQ

**Is Lovable “real” engineering?** Shipping is real engineering. Generated code still needs review, perf passes, and ownership.

**When do I list on Vibecode.game?** When you have a stable URL, honest tool tags, and a short making-of—see [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games).

## Next steps

- Compare other stacks in [AI tools](/ai-tools).
- Find a deadline via [game jams](/game-jams).

---

*YGG Play connects builders, tools, and players in the vibe-coded games space.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Ship Every Jam Build with Claude and GitHub Actions
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/ship-game-jam-build-claude-github-actions
Source: src/content/blog/en/ship-game-jam-build-claude-github-actions.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Turn “it works on my machine” into repeatable artifacts: CI checks, tagged releases, and itch uploads you can trigger from main.

Game jams punish **manual deploys**. The last hour should be **tuning feel**, not watching a stalled upload. Pairing **Claude** (or any strong coding model) with **GitHub Actions** gives you a boring pipeline—exactly what you want when adrenaline is high.

## What a minimal jam CI does

1. **Install** dependencies with a lockfile.
2. **Build** your static export or bundled `dist/`.
3. **Archive** the playable folder as an artifact you can download—or push to itch with `butler` if you use secrets.

You do not need Kubernetes. You need **repeatability**.

## Designing the workflow file

Keep jobs split: `lint` (optional), `build`, `package`. Use concurrency groups so double-pushes cancel stale runs. Cache your package manager directory.

When prompting Claude, include:

- Your **package manager** and **build command**.
- Target **Node version** pinned in `.node-version` or `engines`.
- Whether you deploy **WebGL**, **static HTML**, or **Electron** (Electron needs signing—often out of scope for 48h jams).

## Secrets and itch.io

Store `BUTLER_API_KEY` as an Actions secret. Never echo secrets in logs. Test `butler push` locally once before wiring CI so failures are understandable.

## Branch strategy that survives sleep deprivation

- `main` always **builds green**.
- Jam experiments live in **short-lived branches** merged via PR even if you are solo—PRs force a diff story you can revert.

## Table: when to skip automation

| Situation | Automate? |
|-----------|-----------|
| First hour of jam | No—prototype freely |
| 6h before deadline | Yes—one-button package |
| Post-jam polish | Yes—release tags |

## Common failures

- **Missing lockfile** → CI installs random minors.
- **Case-sensitive paths** → works on Windows, fails on Linux runners.
- **Huge artifacts** → trim unused assets before upload.

## FAQ

**Can Claude write my workflow YAML?** Start from official templates, then ask it to adapt—verify every action pin.

**What if my game is client-only?** Static export to `dist/` + upload is enough; add Lighthouse CI only if you care about perf budgets.

**Where do jams fit in discovery?** Track upcoming events on [game jams](/game-jams) and cross-link your release from your [developer hub entry](/developers).

## Next steps

- Browse [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) for how shipped jams present builds.
- Compare AI assistants in [AI tools](/ai-tools) for your next jam stack.

---

*YGG Play publishes practical pipelines for builders who ship on deadlines.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Testing and Debugging Browser Games You Built with AI
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/test-debug-browser-games-built-with-ai
Source: src/content/blog/en/test-debug-browser-games-built-with-ai.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Make AI-generated code legible: reproducible bugs, DevTools habits, and checklists that survive midnight shipping.

AI accelerates typing, not **observability**. Treat debugging as a **first-class skill**: log with intent, reproduce with scripts, and refuse “it works on my laptop” as a closing statement.

## Build a reproduction recipe

For every bug: **URL**, **browser + version**, **steps**, **expected vs actual**, **console trace**. Paste that into your assistant—models solve faster with receipts.

## DevTools habits

- **Performance panel** for rAF spikes.
- **Network** throttling before blaming “lag.”
- **Mobile emulation** then a **real device** pass.

## Deterministic seeds

Wire a `?seed=123` query or debug overlay to replay RNG-heavy levels. Screenshots from the same seed should match—if not, you have nondeterminism to hunt.

## Automated checks that pay rent

- **Unit tests** for scoring and inventory rules.
- **Smoke test** that boots the game and clicks start.
- **Lint** to catch undefined vars introduced by giant diffs.

## Table: bug classes common in AI prototypes

| Symptom | Likely cause | Quick fix |
|---------|--------------|-----------|
| Works once then blank | Event listener duplication | Teardown on scene change |
| Drift on Safari | `100vh` quirks | Use dvh or measure container |
| Desync multiplayer | Full state spam | Throttle + authoritative server |

## FAQ

**Should AI write tests?** Let it draft—**you** pick invariants worth locking.

**When is logging enough?** Until you have paying players or tournaments—then metrics.

## Next steps

- Browse [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) for polish references.
- Compare assistants in [AI tools](/ai-tools).

---

*YGG Play publishes reliability guides for fast-moving indies.*

================================================================================
## Blog: IP Essentials for Vibe-Coded Games (High Level, Not Legal Advice)
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/vibe-coded-games-ip-essentials
Source: src/content/blog/en/vibe-coded-games-ip-essentials.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Trademarks, music, and AI outputs: where teams ask lawyers—and where common sense saves you first.

**Not legal advice.** If you ship commercially, **hire counsel** in your jurisdiction. This article orients **solo and micro teams** toward the questions lawyers expect you to have already considered.

## Content buckets

- **Code** you wrote vs generated—keep licenses and attribution straight in your repo.
- **Art & audio**—prefer assets with **clear licenses**; track provenance.
- **Names & logos**—trademark conflicts hurt storefront visibility.

## AI outputs

Policies evolve. Maintain **prompt logs** and **model/version notes** for commercial work where contracts require it. When platforms ask **how** something was made, clarity protects you.

## Fan works and jams

Read jam rules **before** using third-party IP—even “obvious” homages.

## When to call a lawyer

- Revenue crosses thresholds you are uncomfortable self-judging.
- You receive **cease/desist** correspondence.
- You sign **publisher** or **platform** agreements.

## FAQ

**CC0 everything?** Possible for jam toys; commercial branding usually needs more thought.

**Where to list safely?** Use honest tags on [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) and keep credits visible.

---

*YGG Play encourages informed builders—not anonymous risk.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Your First Browser Game: Vibe-Coding from Zero to Playable
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/vibe-coding-first-browser-game-beginners-guide
Source: src/content/blog/en/vibe-coding-first-browser-game-beginners-guide.mdx
================================================================================

Description: No engine PhD required—start with HTML canvas or a micro-framework, let AI handle glue code, and finish with something you can share.

**Vibe-coding** is not a single tool—it is a loop: *clarify intent → generate scaffolding → test → tighten prompts → ship a slice*. For your **first browser game**, the goal is not originality; it is **closure**: a URL you are proud to hand someone, running on mobile Safari and desktop Chrome without a lecture.

## Choose your rendering surface

You have three sane starting points:

1. **Canvas 2D** — maximum control, minimal dependencies. Great for arcade prototypes.
2. **DOM + CSS** — surprisingly strong for puzzle and narrative micro-games.
3. **Light framework** (Phaser, Kaboom, Pixi)—faster if you already know one; slower if you are fighting docs.

For week-one success, **canvas or DOM** usually wins because the mental model stays small.

## The one-loop rule

Define **one core loop** that takes under sixty seconds per run: *spawn → interact → resolve → score*. Everything else—menus, skins, audio packs—is **stretch goals** tracked in a “later.md” file you do not open until the loop feels juicy.

## Prompting patterns that actually work

Paste a **fixed template** into your assistant:

- **Goal:** one sentence player fantasy.
- **Constraints:** language, max files, no build step (or Vite only).
- **Acceptance tests:** “Space starts the game,” “collision reduces HP,” “game over shows score.”

Ask for **the smallest diff** that passes tests, not a rewrite. Rewrites burn beginners; diffs teach.

## Performance and guilt-free corners

Ship at **30 FPS** if 60 is fighting you. Use **integer math** where possible. Lazy-load assets. Your first game teaches **habits**; optimization culture comes after you have shipped twice.

## Accessibility and fairness

Even jam games benefit from **keyboard focus**, **color contrast**, and **mute**. Add a single toggle early; players remember thoughtfulness.

## Table: first-game stack shortcuts

| If you want… | Start with… | Avoid on day one… |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Fast movement feel | Canvas rAF loop | Full ECS |
| Grid puzzles | DOM grid + CSS | Custom render pipeline |
| Learning multiplayer | Single-screen hot-seat | Dedicated game server |

## Mistakes we see in beginner listings

- **No build instructions** in the README or itch page.
- **No loop video**—three seconds of GIF beats three paragraphs of lore.
- **Mystery controls**—show them on first paint.

## FAQ

**Do I need TypeScript?** Helpful, not mandatory. If errors confuse you, start JS and migrate hot files later.

**Where does AI help most?** Boilerplate, collision helpers, and refactors—not high-level taste. You still direct tone and pacing.

**How do I join the ecosystem?** Publish your stack honestly on your [developer profile](/developers) and browse [AI tools](/ai-tools) others used.

## What to do this weekend

1. Pick canvas or DOM.
2. Implement the loop with touch + keyboard.
3. Deploy to static hosting.
4. Add your page to [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) when you are ready for players.

---

*YGG Play helps builders ship browser-first games and find players who care how games are made.*

================================================================================
## Blog: Engine & Tooling Updates Web-First Indies Should Watch
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/web-first-indies-engine-tooling-updates
Source: src/content/blog/en/web-first-indies-engine-tooling-updates.mdx
================================================================================

Description: WASM maturity, GPU defaults, and export pipelines—without chasing every changelog.

You cannot read every **release note**—so track **themes**: runtime performance, **bundle size**, **audio**, and **mobile GPU** behavior.

## Themes for 2026-era web games

- **WebGPU availability** improving on Android—test fallbacks.
- **Compression** codecs for textures/audio affecting cold load.
- **Threading models** in WASM ports impacting physics-heavy titles.

## Process

Set a **quarterly calendar reminder** to read your engine’s **breaking changes** section only—skip marketing fluff.

## Builder actions

- Maintain **device lab** of one iPhone + one budget Android.
- Log **load time** beside feature work in changelog.

## Links

Discover what ships in the wild via [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) and compare stacks on [AI tools](/ai-tools).

## FAQ

**Upgrade day one?** Only if security or store compliance demands it.

---

*YGG Play prefers boring upgrade discipline over hype chasing.*

================================================================================
## Blog: WebSockets for Vibe-Coded Games: A Primer Before You Code
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/websockets-multiplayer-vibe-coding-primer
Source: src/content/blog/en/websockets-multiplayer-vibe-coding-primer.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Messages, rooms, ticks, and failure modes—so your first socket project does not become a science project.

**WebSockets** give you a persistent, bidirectional pipe—great for frequent state updates. They are not magic: you still design **schemas**, **authority**, and **failure recovery**.

## Mental model

- **Server** owns truth for competitive games (usually).
- **Clients** send intents (`move`, `jump`), not outcomes (`I scored`).
- **Broadcast** deltas, not the universe—bandwidth is a UX issue.

## Message design

Start JSON for readability; optimize to binary if metrics prove need. Always include **`type`**, **`seq` or `tick`**, and **`roomId`**.

## Rooms and matchmaking v0

Random room codes in URL query are enough for prototypes. Swap to a matchmaker when retention demands it.

## Failure modes

- **Silent disconnects** — heartbeat/ping.
- **Replay floods** — rate limit.
- **Cheaty clients** — validate bounds server-side.

## FAQ

**UDP instead?** Browsers do not expose raw UDP; WebRTC data channels exist but add signaling complexity—graduate when needed.

**Hands-on next?** Try [our Cursor multiplayer sprint](/blog/how-to-multiplayer-game-cursor-two-hours).

## Next steps

- Explore listings in [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games).
- Compare tools at [AI tools](/ai-tools).

---

*YGG Play helps you learn multiplayer without myth-making.*

================================================================================
## Blog: YGG Play and the Vibe-Coded Games Community
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/blog/ygg-play-vibe-coded-games-community
Source: src/content/blog/en/ygg-play-vibe-coded-games-community.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Why we care about transparent listings, builder profiles, and players who read credits.

**YGG Play** backs a simple idea: **players and builders both win** when games are easy to try and **honest about how they were made**. Vibe-coding is a **method**, not a genre—arcade, puzzle, and narrative games all belong.

## What we optimize for

- **Fast play** paths and clear controls.
- **Builder identity** through [developer profiles](/developers).
- **Tool literacy** via [AI tools](/ai-tools) pages you can cite.
- **Deadlines** that ship art via [game jams](/game-jams).

## For listers

Bring a **stable URL**, **media**, and **truthful tags**. We surface games to players who want **new mechanics** and **new workflows**, not only AAA polish.

## For players

Expect **browser-first** experiments alongside downloadable titles. Use filters and follow creators whose taste you trust.

## FAQ

**Is everything AI-generated?** No—**AI-assisted** spans a spectrum; read credits and tool tags.

**How to contribute?** Ship something listable on [vibe-coded games](/vibe-coded-games) and tell your story.

---

*YGG Play grows communities that respect craft and context.*

================================================================================
## Game: Arcade Blitz
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/arcade-blitz
Source: src/content/games/en/arcade-blitz.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Fast arena dodging with combo chains and escalating waves.

Arcade Blitz is a high-tempo arena action game built around movement mastery, fast reads, and combo routing under pressure.

## Gameplay

Each run drops you into compact combat arenas with escalating enemy patterns. You chain dashes, parries, and ability cancels to keep your score multiplier alive while surviving increasingly dense wave compositions.

## Key Features

- Skill-based dodge and counter loop with responsive inputs
- Combo meter that rewards aggressive, clean execution
- Wave modifiers that change enemy behavior every round
- Short-session structure designed for repeat runs and leaderboard climbs

## Why It Stands Out

Arcade Blitz blends classic arcade readability with modern action pacing. Attacks are clearly telegraphed, but decisions still need to happen in split seconds, which keeps each attempt tense and satisfying.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` was used to prototype enemy pattern variants and scoring rules, while `Cursor` accelerated iteration on wave tuning scripts and state-machine refactors.

================================================================================
## Game: Byte Brawlers
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/byte-brawlers
Source: src/content/games/en/byte-brawlers.mdx
================================================================================

Description: 2D team battles with gadgets, ultimates, and map control.

Byte Brawlers is a competitive 2D team fighter focused on coordinated pushes, gadget timing, and objective control.

## Gameplay

Matches are played in fast rounds where two squads contest map objectives while managing cooldowns and ult economy. Team comp decisions matter: bruisers open space, supports sustain pressure, and flankers punish bad positioning.

## Key Features

- 3v3 objective modes with quick round turnover
- Distinct brawler classes with unique ultimates
- Gadget system for zoning, displacement, and setup plays
- Ranked and custom lobbies for competitive or casual sessions

## Why It Stands Out

Unlike pure duel fighters, Byte Brawlers emphasizes communication and tempo swings. Well-timed ult chains can flip a match instantly, making teamwork and adaptation the core skill.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` supported prototype balance notes and matchup documentation, while `GitHub Copilot` sped up ability scripting and UI state handling for loadouts.

================================================================================
## Game: Cipher Heist
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/cipher-heist
Source: src/content/games/en/cipher-heist.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Stealth puzzler where timing and route planning are everything.

Cipher Heist is a stealth-puzzle experience where route planning, timing windows, and system manipulation are the heart of every mission.

## Gameplay

You infiltrate high-security facilities, map patrol loops, and chain silent interactions to reach extraction undetected. Levels are designed as logic spaces, so success comes from observation and clean execution rather than brute force.

## Key Features

- Multi-path stealth sandboxes with layered security systems
- Environmental puzzles tied to cameras, locks, and power grids
- Alert-state escalation that changes enemy routes dynamically
- Mission scoring based on noise, visibility, and completion speed

## Why It Stands Out

Cipher Heist rewards patience and planning. Every successful run feels authored by the player because solutions emerge from understanding systems, not following a single scripted path.

## AI-Assisted Development

`Cursor` helped structure mission scripting and trigger logic, while `Claude` supported puzzle flow reviews to keep challenge curves fair and readable.

================================================================================
## Game: Cosmic Farm
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/cosmic-farm
Source: src/content/games/en/cosmic-farm.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Relaxed farming sim on alien planets with cozy progression.

Cosmic Farm is a cozy farming simulation set across small alien worlds, built for relaxed progression and expressive base customization.

## Gameplay

You cultivate biome-specific crops, befriend local creatures, and automate simple production chains to expand your interplanetary homestead. Sessions are intentionally low-pressure, with optional goals that let you play at your own pace.

## Key Features

- Multiple planets with unique weather and soil mechanics
- Crop, crafting, and habitat systems that interconnect naturally
- Decorative building tools for personalized farm layouts
- Bite-sized quests and seasonal events for steady progression

## Why It Stands Out

Cosmic Farm keeps the loop calm without becoming shallow. It mixes light optimization with cozy worldbuilding so players can choose between efficiency and creativity each session.

## AI-Assisted Development

`Claude` was used to iterate quest text and progression beats, while `Copilot` accelerated implementation of inventory helpers and farming simulation utilities.

================================================================================
## Game: Dungeon Dash
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/dungeon-dash
Source: src/content/games/en/dungeon-dash.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Roguelite runs through trap-heavy dungeons with randomized loot.

Dungeon Dash is a run-based roguelite RPG that combines pattern-driven combat with high-variance loot decisions.

## Gameplay

Each descent is procedurally assembled from combat rooms, trap corridors, and elite encounters. You build around found gear and relic synergies, deciding whether to play safely for consistency or push deeper for rare rewards.

## Key Features

- Procedural dungeon layouts with branching risk/reward paths
- Class-like build identities created through relic combinations
- Boss fights with learnable telegraphs and escalating phases
- Meta progression that unlocks new run modifiers and options

## Why It Stands Out

Dungeon Dash balances accessibility and depth: controls are immediate, but long-term mastery comes from build adaptation and encounter knowledge.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` supported early item and relic ideation, while `Claude` helped refine enemy behavior specs and run-economy balancing notes.

================================================================================
## Game: Forest Keepers
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/forest-keepers
Source: src/content/games/en/forest-keepers.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Co-op defense game where teams protect biomes from corruption.

Forest Keepers is a co-op strategy defense game where squads protect living biomes from corruption waves.

## Gameplay

Teams coordinate resource routes, place defenses, and rotate between lanes as threats evolve in real time. Match success depends on communication, role coverage, and timing defensive cooldowns across multiple fronts.

## Key Features

- Cooperative lane-defense gameplay for up to four players
- Biome-specific objectives that change tactical priorities
- Upgrade trees for structures, utility tools, and support abilities
- Dynamic enemy mutations that force mid-match adaptation

## Why It Stands Out

Forest Keepers blends tactical planning with multiplayer urgency. It rewards teams that share information and adjust quickly instead of relying on one fixed build order.

## AI-Assisted Development

`Copilot` accelerated systems code for wave scheduling and upgrades, while `Cursor` improved iteration speed on co-op state sync and balance passes.

================================================================================
## Game: Mecha Rally
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/mecha-rally
Source: src/content/games/en/mecha-rally.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Arena racing with customizable mechs and physics-heavy boosts.

Mecha Rally is an action-racing hybrid where customizable mechs battle for position across physics-heavy arena tracks.

## Gameplay

Races combine boost management, weapon timing, and terrain interaction. You tune chassis stats between events, then adapt your driving line and combat choices based on track hazards and rival loadouts.

## Key Features

- Arena circuits with shortcuts, hazards, and verticality
- Modular mech customization for speed, armor, and handling
- Mid-race combat tools that create high-risk overtakes
- Event ladders with escalating AI and mixed rule sets

## Why It Stands Out

Mecha Rally treats racing and combat as one system: positioning creates attack opportunities, and offense reshapes race strategy.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` supported early tuning frameworks and event rule concepts, while `Gemini` assisted with telemetry review summaries and physics parameter experimentation.

================================================================================
## Game: Neon Nomads
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/neon-nomads
Source: src/content/games/en/neon-nomads.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Open-world courier missions through a cyberpunk metropolis.

Neon Nomads is an open-world adventure centered on courier contracts, faction reputation, and route mastery in a dense cyberpunk city.

## Gameplay

You accept delivery missions with branching constraints, then navigate districts with different risk profiles, surveillance density, and traffic behavior. Progression unlocks mobility tools and narrative paths tied to the factions you prioritize.

## Key Features

- Large connected city with district-based mission identities
- Reputation system that alters jobs, rewards, and story beats
- Traversal upgrades for speed, stealth, and vertical access
- Optional side contracts that expand world lore and economy depth

## Why It Stands Out

Neon Nomads captures the fantasy of being a high-skill city operator. Movement, planning, and relationship management all feed into a meaningful long-term loop.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` helped draft mission variants and dialogue seeds, `Claude` supported narrative consistency passes, and `Cursor` sped up systemic quest iteration.

================================================================================
## Game: Neon Runner
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/neon-runner
Source: src/content/games/en/neon-runner.mdx
================================================================================

Description: A fast-paced endless runner through a synthwave city. Dodge obstacles, collect pulses, and chase the high score — built with vibe coding and a tight feedback loop.

Neon Runner is all about **flow**: one-thumb controls, instant restarts, and a synthwave presentation tuned for high-speed clarity.

## Gameplay

You sprint through procedural lanes, chaining near-misses and pulse pickups to push your multiplier while avoiding late-run pattern overload. Runs are short, but scoring depth comes from route commitment and split-second correction.

## Key Features

- Endless-runner loop designed for rapid retries
- Score multiplier system that rewards confident movement
- Procedural obstacle grammar for high replay variety
- Daily challenge seeds for competitive high-score sessions

## Why It Stands Out

Neon Runner keeps friction low and mastery high. New players can jump in instantly, while advanced players optimize lines and tempo for leaderboard gains.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` helped prototype level grammar and challenge ideas, while `Cursor` accelerated balancing tools and runtime tuning workflows.

================================================================================
## Game: Pixel Quest
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/pixel-quest
Source: src/content/games/en/pixel-quest.mdx
================================================================================

Description: A cozy dungeon crawler with chunky pixels, secret rooms, and bosses that telegraph every move. Perfect for short sessions and speedrun attempts.

Pixel Quest is a cozy pixel RPG with tight room-scale combat, rewarding exploration, and short-session dungeon progression.

## Gameplay

You clear handcrafted dungeon floors, decode enemy tells, and choose when to spend resources or push one room deeper. Hidden paths and optional objectives reward players who explore carefully instead of rushing the critical route.

## Key Features

- Readable pixel combat with pattern-based boss encounters
- Secret rooms, breakables, and optional mini-objectives
- Gear and charm system that supports different playstyles
- Compact runs ideal for quick sessions and repeat attempts

## Why It Stands Out

Pixel Quest mixes comfort and challenge well: visuals are nostalgic and warm, but encounter design still asks for deliberate timing and smart positioning.

## AI-Assisted Development

`ChatGPT` supported item and encounter ideation, while `Claude` helped tune tutorial pacing and readability for new players.

================================================================================
## Game: Quantum Drift
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/quantum-drift
Source: src/content/games/en/quantum-drift.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Time-bending racing with short tracks and high replay value.

Quantum Drift is a high-speed arcade racer built around time-shift mechanics, precision cornering, and short competitive runs.

## Gameplay

Each track challenges you to alternate between normal pace and controlled time dilation to optimize lines and recover from mistakes. Mastery comes from learning when to spend temporal charge for corner exits versus defensive correction.

## Key Features

- Time-bending driving mechanic with strategic resource use
- Compact tracks designed for leaderboard replayability
- Ghost racing and split tracking for lap optimization
- Vehicle handling profiles for different racing styles

## Why It Stands Out

Quantum Drift feels fresh because it turns time control into a skill expression tool, not a simple boost button. Fast races still leave room for deep optimization.

## AI-Assisted Development

`Claude` supported mechanic documentation and balance notes, while `Cursor` sped up telemetry instrumentation and lap-analysis tooling.

================================================================================
## Game: Skyline Tycoon
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/vibe-coded-games/skyline-tycoon
Source: src/content/games/en/skyline-tycoon.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Build and optimize a floating city economy in short sessions.

Skyline Tycoon is a strategy-simulation game where you design and optimize a floating city economy under shifting demand and resource pressure.

## Gameplay

You zone districts, balance production chains, and manage transit throughput while responding to events that disrupt supply or labor. Sessions are structured for meaningful progress in short bursts, with long-term goals tied to city specialization.

## Key Features

- Layered city-economy simulation with clear feedback loops
- Modular district planning and infrastructure management
- Scenario events that force strategic trade-offs
- Sandbox and goal-driven modes for different player styles

## Why It Stands Out

Skyline Tycoon offers satisfying optimization depth without overwhelming onboarding. It gives players enough data to plan intelligently while keeping the interface readable.

## AI-Assisted Development

`Gemini` helped explore economic event scenarios and balancing narratives, while `Cursor` accelerated simulation-system refactors and UI iteration.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Aria Nguyen
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/aria-nguyen
Source: src/content/developers/en/aria-nguyen.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Aria Nguyen

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am focused on narrative-forward **adventure** experiences set in dense **open-world** spaces that reward curiosity. I love when a player can ignore the critical path for an hour and still discover a story thread, a strange character, or a hidden environmental clue that feels intentional.

**Q2: How does your role as Worldbuilding Lead shape your process?**  
As Worldbuilding Lead, I start with the emotional map of a region before I define mechanics. In practice, that means building landmarks, faction tensions, and local myths first, then designing quests that feel like natural consequences of the world rather than isolated mission prompts.

**Q3: What does your AI-assisted workflow look like day to day?**  
I use **ChatGPT** to brainstorm alternate quest outcomes and optional dialogue arcs, especially when I want to test tonal range quickly. I use **Claude** for long-form lore synthesis and consistency checks across story documents, and **Cursor** to speed up implementation passes for scripting hooks and content tooling.

**Q4: How do adventure and open-world tags influence design decisions?**  
The **adventure** tag pushes me to prioritize pacing, wonder, and narrative payoff, while the **open-world** tag forces me to design for player-driven order of discovery. Every biome, side activity, and traversal route has to carry storytelling weight, even if a player encounters it "out of sequence."

**Q5: Can you share a recent challenge and how you solved it?**  
A recent challenge was making side quests feel meaningful without overwhelming players with checklist fatigue. I solved it by grouping quests into small narrative clusters tied to place identity, then used AI-generated variants as drafts before curating them manually for emotional clarity and thematic cohesion.

**Q6: What are you aiming to learn or build next?**  
I want to build a more reactive open-world narrative layer where NPC memory changes ambient dialogue and quest entrances based on prior player behavior. My goal is to combine handcrafted story direction with AI-assisted prototyping so each adventure still feels authored but more alive.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Ava Martinez
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/ava-martinez
Source: src/content/developers/en/ava-martinez.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Ava Martinez

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am building high-energy games that combine **arcade** immediacy with **endless-runner** momentum, then layer in **fighting** depth and **multiplayer** chaos. I want sessions to be instantly fun in 60 seconds but still reward mastery over weeks.

**Q2: How does your role as Creative Director shape your approach?**  
As Creative Director, I spend a lot of time protecting the core feeling of "speed plus style." I align art, controls, audio, and progression around that promise so every decision supports fast readability, expressive inputs, and replay value for solo and social players.

**Q3: What does your AI-assisted workflow look like each week?**  
I use **ChatGPT** for rapid ideation on event modifiers, combo challenge prompts, and onboarding copy. I use **Cursor** to accelerate prototype iteration with designers and engineers, and **GitHub Copilot** to speed up repetitive gameplay scaffolding while we focus on polish and balancing.

**Q4: How do your preferred genres influence the final design?**  
The **arcade** and **endless-runner** tags keep us strict about low-friction controls and instant restart loops. The **fighting** tag pushes us toward readable combat interactions and spacing decisions, while **multiplayer** requires robust comeback systems and fairness under latency.

**Q5: What is one challenge you recently solved?**  
We had a version where advanced players dominated online rounds too quickly, which hurt retention for newer players. We added dynamic matchmaking rules, clearer telegraphs, and adaptive scoring multipliers, then used AI-supported test scenario generation to validate edge cases before launch.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to ship a seasonal multiplayer mode that blends endless-runner routing decisions with short tactical duels. The long-term plan is a living arcade ecosystem where community-created challenges coexist with curated events and still preserve tight, skill-based game feel.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Elena Garcia
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/elena-garcia
Source: src/content/developers/en/elena-garcia.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Elena Garcia

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am most excited by **simulation** games with transparent systems that still generate surprising outcomes. I also enjoy **strategy** layers where players can recover from mistakes through planning rather than brute-force optimization.

**Q2: How does your role as Simulation Designer guide your process?**  
As a Simulation Designer, I define the rules of the world before content quantity. I map resource flows, friction points, and decision timing so every strategic move has visible trade-offs and players can understand why a system succeeded or failed.

**Q3: How do you use AI tools in your workflow?**  
I use **Gemini** to explore alternate system architectures and stress-test assumptions in early design docs. I use **Cursor** to quickly implement balancing experiments, telemetry hooks, and simulation parameter tools so iteration cycles stay short and measurable.

**Q4: How do simulation and strategy tags affect your design choices?**  
The **simulation** tag keeps me focused on systemic coherence and long-term consequences. The **strategy** tag pushes me to ensure players always have multiple viable paths, with enough information to make deliberate choices instead of guessing hidden formulas.

**Q5: Can you share a challenge you recently solved?**  
We had a late-game economy that became too stable, so player decisions stopped feeling meaningful. I reworked sink-and-source timing, introduced regional constraints, and used AI-assisted scenario generation to test unusual play patterns before final balancing.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build a strategy simulation where civilian behavior, logistics, and weather all interact without overwhelming the player. The key goal is a clean interface that communicates deep systems clearly, so complexity feels empowering instead of opaque.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Ethan Walker
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/ethan-walker
Source: src/content/developers/en/ethan-walker.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Ethan Walker

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am focused on team-first **strategy** games where communication and role clarity decide outcomes. I am especially interested in **multiplayer** experiences that let squads improvise under pressure instead of following rigid meta scripts.

**Q2: How does your role as Technical Producer influence development?**  
As Technical Producer, I connect design ambition with delivery reality. I help teams sequence features, define technical constraints early, and protect performance budgets so multiplayer systems stay stable while strategy depth continues to grow.

**Q3: What does your AI-assisted workflow look like?**  
I use **Copilot** for fast implementation support on repetitive systems and integration tasks. I use **Cursor** to accelerate prototype branches, debugging passes, and balancing tools so designers can test strategic changes without long engineering bottlenecks.

**Q4: How do your preferred genres shape game decisions?**  
The **strategy** tag means we prioritize meaningful information, counterplay, and long-term planning. The **multiplayer** tag means we must design around latency tolerance, communication affordances, and fair recovery opportunities for teams that fall behind early.

**Q5: What is one challenge you recently solved?**  
We had a cooperative mode where one role became mandatory, reducing team composition creativity. We rebuilt utility distribution across classes, adjusted cooldown interactions, and used AI-assisted test matrix generation to validate more balanced team archetypes.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to ship a modular multiplayer strategy framework where seasonal updates can add new team objectives without destabilizing core systems. The long-term vision is deeper strategic expression with faster balancing cycles and stronger competitive integrity.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Liam Chen
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/liam-chen
Source: src/content/developers/en/liam-chen.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Liam Chen

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I love blending **arcade** clarity with **RPG** progression and **roguelite** unpredictability. My favorite projects are ones where each run feels immediately readable but character builds and encounter outcomes still surprise experienced players.

**Q2: How does your role as Gameplay Engineer shape your workflow?**  
As Gameplay Engineer, I focus on making mechanics feel precise under pressure. I care about input timing, combat readability, and encounter cadence, then I reinforce those systems with progression logic that makes each upgrade choice meaningful.

**Q3: What does your AI-assisted process look like in practice?**  
I use **ChatGPT** to draft alternate combat rules, status-effect interactions, and reward loop ideas during concept phases. I use **Claude** to reason through complex balancing edge cases and to refine implementation plans before coding high-impact changes.

**Q4: How do arcade, RPG, and roguelite tags influence your design decisions?**  
The **arcade** tag keeps controls crisp and instantly understandable, while the **RPG** tag pushes long-term build identity. The **roguelite** tag ensures failure still teaches something useful, with procedural variation that encourages adaptation rather than repetition.

**Q5: Can you share a recent challenge and solution?**  
We saw players converging on one dominant build too early in progression. I reworked item synergies, adjusted risk-reward pacing across runs, and used AI-assisted simulation prompts to identify outlier combinations before updating live balance.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build a combat sandbox where arcade responsiveness and roguelite variety coexist with deeper RPG buildcraft. The next milestone is a smarter encounter director that reacts to player style without sacrificing fairness or readability.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Marcus Reed
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/marcus-reed
Source: src/content/developers/en/marcus-reed.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Marcus Reed

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am focused on skill-heavy **racing** games with the immediacy of classic **arcade** design. I want every lap to teach players something about risk, route selection, and momentum management without burying that learning under unnecessary complexity.

**Q2: How does your role as Gameplay Programmer influence your approach?**  
As Gameplay Programmer, I obsess over feel before feature count. I iterate on handling curves, traction behavior, and boost timing until inputs feel trustworthy, then layer progression systems that reward consistency and experimentation.

**Q3: What does your AI workflow look like day to day?**  
I use **Claude** to think through tuning logic and to evaluate edge-case behavior in vehicle systems. I use **Cursor** for rapid iteration on gameplay scripts, race-state tools, and debug instrumentation so I can test many handling hypotheses quickly.

**Q4: How do racing and arcade tags guide design choices?**  
The **racing** tag demands clean competitive readability and strong track identity. The **arcade** tag pushes me toward low-friction onboarding, quick rematches, and satisfying speed feedback so players can enjoy the loop immediately while still mastering deeper mechanics.

**Q5: What challenge did you recently solve?**  
One track had a shortcut that dominated all leaderboard routes and reduced strategic variety. I rebalanced split times, adjusted collision forgiveness, and used AI-assisted scenario planning to validate more route diversity without removing high-skill expression.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build a racing toolkit that supports dynamic weather and surface conditions while keeping controls predictable. The goal is to create arcade-accessible racing that still supports long-term mastery and competitive scene depth.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Maya Patel
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/maya-patel
Source: src/content/developers/en/maya-patel.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Maya Patel

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am most excited by games that merge logical **puzzle** structures with high-tension **stealth** play. I love experiences where every room is a problem space and every solution reflects the player’s planning style.

**Q2: How does your role as Systems Designer shape your process?**  
As a Systems Designer, I focus on readable rule sets that create many valid strategies. I design guard behavior, visibility logic, tool affordances, and objective layering so players can improvise while still understanding consequence and risk.

**Q3: How do AI tools fit into your daily workflow?**  
I use **Cursor** to prototype systemic interactions and quickly test small rule changes in stealth scenarios. I use **Claude** for design reasoning, edge-case exploration, and narrative variant drafting before I curate and tighten each level’s authored intent.

**Q4: How do puzzle and stealth tags influence design decisions?**  
The **puzzle** tag means clarity is non-negotiable: players must be able to read systems and form hypotheses. The **stealth** tag requires controlled tension, predictable enemy logic, and elegant fail states that encourage adaptation rather than frustration.

**Q5: Can you share a challenge you recently solved?**  
We had a level where players felt forced into one route, which reduced both stealth creativity and puzzle satisfaction. I added alternate timing windows, tool-based bypasses, and AI-assisted test-path generation to ensure multiple viable strategies.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build a stealth puzzle framework where mission spaces evolve in response to player behavior across chapters. The next step is stronger systemic reactivity while maintaining the clear mental models that make puzzle-driven stealth rewarding.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Noah Kim
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/noah-kim
Source: src/content/developers/en/noah-kim.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Noah Kim

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am excited by high-speed **arcade** titles with the intensity of modern **action** combat. I want players to feel immediate control confidence, then discover deeper timing and spacing layers as they push difficulty.

**Q2: How does your role as Lead Designer shape your approach?**  
As Lead Designer, I define the core interaction loop first and protect it through every production phase. I align UX, animation timing, and level pacing around that loop so the action feels readable, punchy, and consistently rewarding.

**Q3: What does your AI-assisted workflow look like?**  
I use **ChatGPT** to generate mechanic variants, tutorial approaches, and encounter ideas during exploration phases. I use **Cursor** for rapid implementation and iteration on prototype logic, then we validate decisions with telemetry and hands-on playtesting.

**Q4: How do arcade and action tags influence your design decisions?**  
The **arcade** tag pushes us toward low friction, instant retries, and clear feedback loops. The **action** tag demands stronger enemy readability, responsive controls, and encounter escalation that tests skill rather than just reaction speed.

**Q5: Can you share a challenge and how you solved it?**  
Our mid-game difficulty ramp was too steep, causing churn before players reached advanced mechanics. I restructured encounter sequencing, improved visual telegraphs, and used AI-assisted scenario drafting to test alternate progression curves quickly.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build an action-forward arcade platform where movement tech, combo routing, and boss scripting all reinforce one skill language. The goal is a game that feels approachable on day one and deeply expressive by week three.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Sofia Rossi
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/sofia-rossi
Source: src/content/developers/en/sofia-rossi.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Sofia Rossi

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am most excited by gentle **simulation** games with a warm **casual** rhythm that supports daily play. I want players to feel cared for by the systems: clear goals, low pressure, and meaningful progress through small moments.

**Q2: How does your role as Product Designer influence your process?**  
As Product Designer, I design for emotional continuity across sessions. I focus on interface tone, progression pacing, and reward cadence so the simulation remains engaging without demanding intense mechanical precision from casual players.

**Q3: What does your AI-assisted workflow look like?**  
I use **Claude** to shape content frameworks, narrative micro-copy, and consistency checks for long-form progression arcs. I use **Copilot** to accelerate implementation details in UI and systems tooling, then rely on structured playtests for final tuning.

**Q4: How do simulation and casual tags influence design decisions?**  
The **simulation** tag keeps me focused on coherent world rules and long-term systems depth. The **casual** tag reminds me to reduce friction everywhere: readable onboarding, forgiving mechanics, and progression that feels steady instead of punishing.

**Q5: What challenge have you recently solved?**  
We noticed players loved early sessions but dropped once management complexity increased too quickly. I rebalanced feature unlock order, simplified decision surfaces, and used AI-assisted content pass planning to keep depth while preserving the cozy casual tone.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build a simulation game where personalization and seasonal content keep casual players returning for months. The next milestone is a stronger live-content pipeline that scales variety without diluting clarity or emotional warmth.

================================================================================
## Developer profile: Zoe Park
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/developers/zoe-park
Source: src/content/developers/en/zoe-park.mdx
================================================================================

### Simulated Interview with Zoe Park

**Q1: What kind of games are you most excited to build right now?**  
I am excited by competitive projects that combine the momentum of **racing** with the intensity of **action** game design. I want speed to be tactical, where movement choices and split-second decisions matter as much as raw reflexes.

**Q2: How does your role as Game Director shape your process?**  
As Game Director, I define a clear gameplay thesis and keep every discipline aligned to it. I spend a lot of time on decision framing: what to prototype, what to cut, and how to preserve a cohesive player identity from first lap to high-level play.

**Q3: How do you use AI in your day-to-day workflow?**  
I use **ChatGPT** for early concept exploration, mode ideation, and communication framing for cross-team alignment. I use **Gemini** to compare strategic design alternatives and evaluate system trade-offs before we commit to expensive production decisions.

**Q4: How do racing and action tags influence your design choices?**  
The **racing** tag means precision handling, readable track language, and skill-based route planning. The **action** tag pushes us toward stronger encounter tension, high-impact feedback, and more expressive risk-reward moments during high-speed play.

**Q5: Can you share a recent challenge and solution?**  
We had a prototype where spectacle was strong but competitive clarity was weak in crowded matches. I led a redesign of camera rules, collision communication, and visual priority systems, then used AI-assisted review checklists to speed up iteration across teams.

**Q6: What are you aiming to build next?**  
I want to build a racing-action experience with esports-grade readability and broad player accessibility. The next target is a mode structure that supports both quick public matches and long-term mastery for highly competitive players.

================================================================================
## AI tool: Bolt
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/ai-tools/bolt
Source: src/content/ai-tools/en/bolt.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Bolt.new lets teams generate and iterate full-stack apps from prompts directly in the browser.

## Why creators use Bolt

Bolt is useful for rapidly prototyping game landing pages, dashboards, and companion web experiences without local setup.

## Tutorials

- [Bolt.new](https://bolt.new/)
- [StackBlitz docs](https://developer.stackblitz.com/)
- [Prompting tips for app generation](https://developer.stackblitz.com/platform/webcontainers)

## AI tool: Claude
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/ai-tools/claude
Source: src/content/ai-tools/en/claude.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Anthropic's AI assistant for long-context reasoning, coding help, and reliable writing workflows.

## Why creators use Claude

Claude is strong at planning game features, producing structured docs, and iterating on prompt-based gameplay content.

## Tutorials

- [Claude prompt engineering guide](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)
- [Claude API quickstart](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/initial-setup)
- [Anthropic developer docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/)

================================================================================
## AI tool: Cursor
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/ai-tools/cursor
Source: src/content/ai-tools/en/cursor.mdx
================================================================================

Description: AI-first editor for code generation, refactoring, repository understanding, and agent-assisted development.

## Why creators use Cursor

Cursor helps indie developers go from game idea to working feature quickly with chat, edit, and agent workflows.

## Tutorials

- [Cursor docs](https://docs.cursor.com/)
- [Rules and project context](https://docs.cursor.com/context/rules)
- [Agent workflow basics](https://docs.cursor.com/agent/overview)

## Video walkthrough

<iframe
  width="100%"
  height="420"
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/sPzB5f7l8vA"
  title="Cursor tutorial"
  frameborder="0"
  allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share"
  allowfullscreen
></iframe>

================================================================================
## AI tool: Lovable
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/ai-tools/lovable
Source: src/content/ai-tools/en/lovable.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Prompt-first app builder focused on shipping polished product interfaces and integrations quickly.

## Why creators use Lovable

Lovable is helpful for rapidly creating game launch pages, onboarding flows, and admin tools from natural-language prompts.

## Tutorials

- [Lovable home](https://lovable.dev)
- [Lovable guide](https://docs.lovable.dev/)
- [Building with prompts](https://docs.lovable.dev/getting-started)

## AI tool: OpenAI
URL: https://www.vibecode.game/ai-tools/openai
Source: src/content/ai-tools/en/openai.mdx
================================================================================

Description: OpenAI provides GPT models and APIs for ideation, coding, content generation, and production-ready AI workflows.

## Why creators use OpenAI

OpenAI tools are widely used by game creators for brainstorming mechanics, generating content drafts, prototyping systems, and accelerating iteration across design and engineering tasks.

## Tutorials

- [OpenAI API quickstart](https://platform.openai.com/docs/quickstart)
- [Text generation guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)
- [OpenAI developer docs](https://platform.openai.com/docs/overview)

URL: https://www.vibecode.game/ai-tools/replit
Source: src/content/ai-tools/en/replit.mdx
================================================================================

Description: Cloud coding environment with AI assistance, collaborative editing, and one-click deploy workflows.

## Why creators use Replit

Replit makes it easy to build prototypes, test game logic, and share working demos instantly with teammates.

## Tutorials

- [Replit docs](https://docs.replit.com/)
